sentences.text: My name is Sy Sterns.
First name is S-Y S-E-T-E-R-N-S.
E-R-N-S.
At birth was the same name.
My name is the same.
Schsan.
Sternschsan.
S-T-E-R-N-S- -- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] C-H-S-A-N.
I forgot.
No.
Simcha.
Simcha.
Ben Yaakov.
Yeah.
I had another name, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Shlomo Simon Mastowitz.
That name I had for a period from 1946 till-- till I got citizenship was 1955.
Yeah, became a United States citizen.

The circumstances-- when we went in Czechoslovakia to the border, we had to take a different name because the camp that we were in Bad Reichenhall, they shipped the younger people-- they went to Israel.
And the Germans wouldn't have-- they don't want no more PD, displaced person.
So we took their identity.
And everybody was getting a-- from UNRRA a package.
And they will-- they were coming for them-- we were getting the packages.
And this name stayed with it.
There's a lot of people like that.
I had for the camp, had it, that name.
Because then all of them people, when they left for Israel, they replaced them with younger people.
And the older people stayed there for a while.
He is in Israel.
[INAUDIBLE]
No.
But I'm just 73 till October, eh?
Oh, OK.
In Mzhirech.
M-Z-H-I-R-E-C-H.
It's in-- by [NON-ENGLISH]
In Poland.
The town was a small town.
And all the Jewish people lived in the center of the city.
And the Gentiles lived in, like, in the suburbs, and along the outskirts of the city.
And as far-- all the business people were Jewish, and the Gentiles were farmers.
I would think about 2,000 families.
What are families?
What do we consider?
Two people, three, or four.
You know, there's a big difference.
I don't know.
An average family was four people.
Four people in average.
At least four.
Some had five.
Well, like I say, the-- this-- I would say about 80%.
And I think from the 80%, and when the war was over, 150 people was alive.
And 50 of them were the people where they went in the woods, that part of them.
That's it.
Yeah.
No.
No.
We were freemen but not Hasidim.
Our family was three children.
I was the middle child.
And my brother was Yitzchok and Simcha and my sister was Sosie.
My parents' name was Yakov Ben Elihezer Sternsczs.
And my mother was Rifke Bat Itzhak-- Bat Itzhak.
And she was born in Brezhnev.
My father was born in Miedzyrzecz.
A shadkhan.
I remember they tell me.
I think my father was older.
And there was a shadkhan, and they matched them up.
Well, they-- yeah, I guess so.
Yeah.
I think maybe they were not poor people so-- and my mother was [INAUDIBLE] she come from-- not from a poor family either.
So it just worked out.
Yeah.
Yeah, most of the time.
It's like every other family sometime.
They had certain disagreements too, though.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But most of the time was-- was pretty good.
In Europe, they didn't know nothing.
They only know how to work, and go to shul, and no vacation, no goynish.
That's it.
Yontif you got, you know, but I mean, they was rarely observing, like, all of the Jewish holidays.
Only when she bentsh licht.
I mean, for Shabbos or a yontif.
But she didn't always, no.
Yeah.
Yeah, the temple-- not every temple had mikvehs though.
Uh-uh.
Usually most of the time the men did go.
Because I remember I was younger, you know.
And I did go in the mikveh.
But I-- I never hear that about-- so they said, maybe there were some, but not too many women need to go to the mikveh.
Maybe you're right.
But I know my mother didn't go.
I didn't go.
No.
I think he had that little mustache, yeah.
No.
No payos.
No, didn't-- when he were in the business, he didn't wear no hat.
On Shabbos when we go-- when we eat a meal, he wear a yarmulke and make a bracha and then we seated.
And Friday night, the same.
No, my-- my-- my zayde did.
He did wear a kapota.
He was more-- he was-- he was more-- because the business-- it was my zayde's business.
And he had two sons.
And one-- the other son was in Russia.
So the two-- the business left by him for my father and Tzike.
And he got kind of older.
And he said he don't want to-- He used to come still there, but he was not too active.
And what happened then, yeah, then the brothers had kind of an argument.
And they split up.
He had to pay him out so much.
And he had-- it's in the same business but maybe-- and let's see.
How many?
Maybe about 10 businesses, I think he opened up.
And they were-- he was successful too.
What kind of business?
Hardware business.
I mean, we had the hardware business, and then we had the factory that was making farm machinery.
And after that they made the machine shop near the house.
And I think it was a half a block.
He was then working.
He had to machine it and then assemble it.
And they'd be gone for a long time, but I think towards the end, I think we liquidated.
There wasn't enough profit in that.
He was born there too, I guess.
No, he didn't-- he didn't pursue that.
He was very religious.
He used to go and-- every day, three times a day he used to go davening.
And then they used the Talmud.
They read and learned in that.
I remember one thing they used to check me if I have the [NON-ENGLISH] tzitzit.
And I seen they are on this side, I run across the street.
But they see me, say, come over, come over.
What happened to the tzitze.
[LAUGHING]
I said, I forgot it.
[LAUGHING] OK, next time you wear the-- wear the [NON-ENGLISH] you know the [NON-ENGLISH] I wear the tzitzit.
But, I mean, sometime, you know, when you play ball, it's just too warm to wear it though.
You're a little kid, you know.
No, I never wore a yarmulke.
I wore it going to shul, yeah.
I used to go to every Friday and Saturday night.
No, Friday night and Saturday.
Not Saturday night.
And a lot of times, we used to bring in a guest just about quite a few times, like Friday night, and Shabbos-- and then-- yeah, for Shabbos.
But they didn't sleep at our store.
I think they slept in the temple.
Yeah, sometimes, like, it's a couple of times we had even two people.
The house.
The house was a house with a kitchen, and a living room, and a dining room and two bedrooms.
That was considered a big house.
Yeah, it was a big house.
And I remember one time I had washed the floor, and I slipped on the-- I was-- I was skiing like, you know, it's wet.
And I broke my ankle.
And I had to have this tire iron by the bed.
You know, they have weight.
And I had to stay laying in the bed.
I remember like no, oh, my gosh-- at least six weeks in the bed.
And you couldn't, you know, different.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, you have weight, and they drilled a hole, I remember, in the bed.
And they put all the weight on and the-- and the ankle and the-- There are certain things you remember.
But can I use the-- my-- my eye is running a little.
Can I use that?
It's allergies or whatever.
OK, that's better.
It runs on one side.
The house was furnished-- let's see.
In the living room was-- let's start from the bedroom.
There was two beds.
In the other one was one bed.
And then I had a bed.
And I usually slept on the-- what do you call it?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know where you-- where you-- where you put the-- the coals in there?
The [INAUDIBLE] chuck in the kitchen--
No.
It-- it-- right.
It goes through the house.
And I slept-- I had a bed moved over there.
And so I always stayed warm.
You know, in Europe, no matter how much you were worked, you still were cold.
I had everybody one time in my bed.
I said, no, no.
And were in the living room, we have an, a big buffet-- two.
One on the bottom.
One on the top one.
And a table.
And I think there was six chairs, I guess.
Yeah.
Well, we were the first one really.
We were selling-- we were the first one to put electricity.
I think it was 1937.
I mean, after that time, after that time we didn't have electricity, and the town didn't have it.
But they were selling it.
And then, we were selling it in the store to people that wanted it.
And after that time, there was lamps, you know, gasoline lamps.
No, not indoor plumbing.
No indoor water.
I remember I used to carry the water on my shoulder.
Then, you go to the well, and-- and then you have barrels.
And then, you dump the water.
It was a job.
I mean, I was young and didn't mind to do that though.
We have for me.
Not for my older brother or my daughter because--
Only for me that they had one-- a governess.
She just-- all what she did is took care of me.
That was her job.
Well, most of the time, my mother was there.
But, I mean, she didn't-- in the kitchen, she didn't-- she was cleaning and taking care of me.
And that's about all she did.
I don't remember.
But I remember she used to come even-- even she got married, she didn't have no children.
She used to come and check up on me, see how I'm doing.
But otherwise-- well, she helped me out during the war a little.
But always she couldn't do I suppose.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Someone else who went [INAUDIBLE] really.
Very observant.
Like Shabbos well, Shabbos-- well, you remember, like, we had that store.
And the Shabbos in-- like the night before, and he says, close the store.
He used to klopf at the stechen.
He had to close the door.
And my father's rooms-- he says, let's take everything in there.
He wasn't ready for the second time.
And the Shabbos, you know, when Shabbos was, the store was closed.
From Friday-- Friday till that's it.
And the whole town closed down.
All the Jewish people.
You could-- you're not supposed to sell on Sunday, but some customer, they come to the house.
And I remember I used to stay outside, and when the policeman was not there, we'd let him in.
And then later, we'd let him out.
Otherwise, there was a fine for opening.
No business could be open on Saturday.
No.
Yeah.
I had a radio.
I told you that-- what was that?
Telefunken?
Yeah, Telefunken.
And I remember the news.
Everybody-- some of the neighbors were staying in the window and listening to that.
Because not everybody had a radio.
No.
I don't know.
Next door was some business.
What was it?
It was a bank.
Yeah, was a bank there.
And they had a telephone.
Nobody had telephones.
My favorite holiday-- Pesach.
Oh, they used to make all kinds of [NON-ENGLISH] and all kind of-- [NON-ENGLISH].
And I think it used to take like six, no, 46 [INAUDIBLE] and Purim and Pesach to get everything that they would take and paint everything.
This was a big-- a big holiday.
I helped to take down the dishes.
All the dishes were in the attic.
And you had to put a-- a ladder and crawl up.
And like three, four people pass on all the dishes.
And then after it's over, you got to put them back up.
Every-- every year.
I know they tell me to be careful.
These dishes are 100 years old.
Don't drop it.
So I know it sounded they were-- were leave from generation to generation.
I can't recall what they look.
No, I can't.
To eat.
What did they make?
They make like Chunt.
I liked that Chunt.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, in the house.
I mean, matter of fact, in the beginning, I remember my mother was baking even challah.
And she was baking challah.
But she was baking bread too.
But later, a bakery come in town, and then she didn't bake no bread.
But she still challah, and she used to get up, like, Friday 4 o'clock in the morning, again, have to put the yeast in that.
And then all kind of thing.
She used to bake for our Shabbos though.
I went in [NON-ENGLISH] Tarbut, I guess.
And now, I get it.
And after all the years, it come to-- back to me.
It was-- it was secular Hebrew.
But you had to have, I think, one hour or two hours in Polish and all the other one was in Hebrew.
All the other.
In the beginning, no.
Later on, yes.
I don't know.
Something-- I was-- I was not motivated enough.
He did-- it was not-- well, let's see.
How could I put-- I was bored with it.
But later on, it got better.
I'd say I liked science.
Science, I liked.
What I knew were they-- they were not teaching-- they were teaching, like, let's see.
What were they teaching, most of it?
Hebrew.
And then they were teaching, like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you call it from the-- what do you call it in the [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Chumash, yeah.
And Tanakh.
Talmud.
Yeah.
Yeah, no, without commentators.
What?
What is Rashi?
I'm forgetting.
Yes, soccer.
No, I was too chubby.
I couldn't run after the ball.
[LAUGHING] But I still played though.
I had fun, and I played.
My bar mitzvah, yeah.
I think I'm born in 15 day at Thomas.
But now, I forget that what-- what Haftarah is.
I forgot.
I should have called the rabbi and asked him what Haftarah was.
Yeah.
To the bimah.
And then--
Yeah, the Haftarah.
Well, there they don't do it like here.
They do it like-- oop.
They do-- they do the Haftarah.
They, I mean, you're 13 here, they don't let you to the bimah.
There's special people for that.
It's not like over here.
When you're 13 years, you can pray.
You can go on the bimah.
Over there, you're a good bar mitzvah, you say Haftarah.
In shul, you have to bring all kind of-- in shul, make a party.
And then in the afternoon, another party at home.
You know, the neighbors.
This was strictly for the temple, when I was there.
See I remember little thing-- the [INAUDIBLE].
That's it.
Don't remember.
I talked to my brother, and we sat down both and don't--
Yeah, a little stuff, not too much really, you know.
People were-- most of the people that come to a bar mitzvah, they-- they cooked and that.
And everybody bring things.
And-- But I remember the bar mitzvah lasted from-- from noon all the way till the evening.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I remember that's about-- what I know?
I don't remember all of that.
I don't know.
Your friends-- were your friends were all Jewish--
All Jewish.
Only they didn't-- in school we didn't wear no-- no kippahs.
Just--
But they were all Jewish.
All Jewish.
I didn't have any Gentile friend.
A Gentle friend, my only memory-- I know it weren't-- the shoemaker was Gentile.
[INAUDIBLE]
No.
They only-- they only used to come and buy.
And I think every Thursday was like a farmers' market.
I personally didn't have much because we stuck all together in the same group.
My parents either because they were-- had their own business.
Later, through the years, it got worse though.
I think-- when was it-- in 1935, and I-- that's when I could hear it already.
Yeah.
I hear that he's-- he's rounding up Jewish people and sending them out from the-- in the German news because at that time, Poland was not occupied.
It was some commentator from England.
From England, commentators through the radio.
BB, what is it?
BBC?

No, there's-- they were talking in some other [INAUDIBLE] in a different language where they could-- BBC usually broadcast in all kind of languages so they could pick it up in their language what they knew.
I was-- I was-- when I was young, I was really scared.
I was a kid.
And, yes, the parents, and they calm you down.
Ah, that's nothing.
And you know, you just-- and you hear all of the bad things, and you're a little kid.
You're kind of afraid about it.
But they calm you down, the parents.
Oh, he dare not come in here.
It's only there.
You know how things are.
Can I drink a little water?
I got to have permission, you know, Ari.
And when did you start to take it seriously?
When I started taking it seriously, in '39.
In '39 when I could-- win the-- and the-- and this, the Russian took over one part.
And then the refugees from-- from Poland start to come in to us, and we have to take in-- every family had to take in two or three people, I think.
Matter of fact, we took in a couple-- people from the other side of Poland, you know.
They-- they said they are-- they are killing people, and they're putting them in jail.
And that they can raid our businesses.
And it was no good though.
But still, people didn't believe it.
